submission_summary.coverage_requested: Primary Commercial General Liability (ISO CG 00 01), Products/Completed Operations, Umbrella/Excess Liability ($5M), and Employers Liability. Occurrence form preferred with per-location and per-project aggregates where available.
submission_summary.description_of_operations:
Cascade Precision Fabricators, Inc. is a privately held metal fabrication and light assembly manufacturer specializing in small- to mid-volume production runs for industrial and technology clients in the Pacific Northwest. The company was founded in 2008 and operates out of a 42,000 square foot leased facility in an established industrial park in Portland, Oregon. Operations run two shifts, Monday through Friday, with occasional overtime on Saturdays during peak demand.

Primary operations include: CNC laser cutting, CNC press brake forming, MIG/TIG welding, light machining (drilling, tapping, milling of small components), hardware insertion, and final assembly of subcomponents (e.g., electrical enclosures, machine guards, brackets, and custom frames). Approximately 70% of revenue is from contract fabrication to customer specifications, 20% from proprietary product lines (standardized electrical control cabinets and machine guarding systems), and 10% from on-site measurement and installation support for local customers.

The vast majority of products are components or subassemblies that are incorporated into customers’ finished equipment and are not consumer-facing. Typical end uses include industrial automation equipment, food processing machinery, clean-room enclosures for electronics manufacturing, and custom material-handling systems. No work is performed on aircraft, watercraft, automotive safety systems, medical implants, or products intended for direct human support (e.g., scaffolding for public use, playground equipment). The company does not perform any residential construction or installation work.

The insured employs 38 full-time staff: 1 president, 1 operations manager, 1 office/HR manager, 2 engineers/estimators, 4 lead fabricators, 20 shop floor fabricators/welders/machine operators, 4 assemblers, 3 shipping/receiving personnel, and 2 drivers. All fabrication employees receive documented safety training, including lockout/tagout, PPE use, machine guarding, forklift operation (for designated employees), and welding fume/eye protection procedures. A written safety manual was updated in March 2024 and is reviewed annually.

The facility includes a segregated welding area with local exhaust ventilation, a machining area with fixed machine guards and emergency stops, a small paint touch-up booth for non-bulk aerosol and small-quantity touch-up painting (no large-scale spray painting), and a shipping area with two dock doors. No galvanizing, plating, or large-scale painting/coating is performed in-house; these services are subcontracted to specialized vendors who carry their own insurance.

Finished goods are packaged on pallets and shipped via LTL freight carriers or delivered locally by company-owned box trucks. Approximately 95% of sales are within the United States (primarily OR, WA, CA, and ID) and 5% are to Canada. There are no sales to Europe or Asia, and no products are knowingly exported to sanctioned countries. All products are manufactured to customer-approved drawings, and the company maintains job travelers, inspection records, and material certifications for traceability.

The insured has not acquired any other companies and does not have any subsidiaries. There is no exposure to blasting, demolition, hazardous waste, or environmental remediation. There is minimal public foot traffic at the premises (occasional customer visits and vendor deliveries) with visitors required to check in at the front office and wear appropriate PPE when entering the shop floor. The president, Michael Alvarez, is heavily involved in day-to-day operations and personally chairs the monthly safety committee meeting.
submission_summary.exposures_line_1: Products/completed operations exposure from custom metal fabricated components and proprietary electrical enclosures used in industrial machinery and automation systems.
submission_summary.exposures_line_2: Premises liability exposure from 42,000 sq ft fabrication facility with limited vendor and customer visits, shipping/receiving docks, forklift traffic, and small paint touch-up area.
submission_summary.exposures_line_3: Auto and off-premises exposure from two company-owned box trucks and occasional on-site measurement/installation support at customer industrial locations (no residential work).
submission_summary.iso_benchmark_rating: ISO GL class 44709 (Metal Goods Mfg – NOC) and related schedule classes, current carrier using manual rates with 0.92 schedule credit based on favorable loss history and strong safety controls.

loss_history.details_on_claims_over_25k:
Policy Term 2016–2017 – Products/Completed Operations Claim – Machine Guarding Bracket Failure

Date of Loss: 2016-11-03

Description: A custom-fabricated steel bracket used to support a light-duty machine guard on a customer’s automated packaging line fractured during operation. The bracket was part of a batch of 60 pieces fabricated to the customer’s drawing. When the bracket failed, a hinged polycarbonate guard panel became dislodged and struck a machine operator’s forearm. The operator sustained a non-displaced hairline fracture and soft-tissue injury. The customer alleged that the bracket design, as manufactured by Cascade Precision Fabricators, was insufficient to handle the dynamic load of repeated opening/closing of the guard.

Allegations and Handling: The claim was tendered to Cascade’s GL carrier as a bodily injury and property damage claim under products/completed operations. The claimant (employee of the customer) pursued damages for medical expenses, lost wages (four weeks off work), and pain and suffering. The customer also sought reimbursement for line downtime and emergency field repairs. Investigation determined that Cascade fabricated the bracket strictly to the customer’s supplied CAD drawing and material specifications (10-gauge cold-rolled steel, single bend, no gusset). However, there was some ambiguity in the drawing related to the radius at the bend, which may have contributed to stress concentration.

Outcome and Payments: The insurer appointed defense counsel and an engineering expert. After review, it was determined that while the customer’s design was marginal, Cascade could have flagged the potential stress issue given its experience with similar brackets. To avoid protracted litigation and preserve the commercial relationship, the insurer negotiated a settlement. Total incurred was $32,750, broken down roughly as follows: $19,500 indemnity to the injured worker (inclusive of medical reimbursement and lost wages), $7,250 for the customer’s claimed downtime and emergency reinforcement work, and $6,000 allocated to defense and expert engineering fees. No subrogation was pursued. The file is closed with no open reserve.

Corrective Actions: Following this loss, Cascade implemented several changes:
1) Engineering Review Protocol: Any customer-supplied design involving load-bearing brackets, guards, or supports is now subject to a documented internal engineering review. If potential weaknesses are identified, Cascade issues a written design concern notice to the customer and requests confirmation or revised drawings before production.
2) Material and Thickness Guidelines: Internal design guidelines were created outlining minimum material thickness, bend radii, and reinforcement requirements for common bracket types and guard supports, based on span length and expected load.
3) Traceability and Labeling: The company enhanced job traveler documentation to clearly note when a part is manufactured exactly per customer print versus with mutually agreed design modifications.
4) Customer Education: For key accounts, Cascade’s engineers now participate in periodic design review meetings to discuss manufacturability and safety considerations for new projects.

Since implementing these measures, Cascade has not experienced any similar bracket or guard-related claims, and customer feedback on the new review process has been positive.

underwriter_notes.underwriter_notes:
Account appears to be a well-managed light-to-medium hazard manufacturing risk with a stable 15+ year operating history and no indication of rapid, uncontrolled growth or recent acquisitions. Management is experienced and engaged in safety oversight, with a documented safety program and regular committee meetings.

Loss history over the past 5 policy terms is favorable with low frequency and one notable severity loss in 2016–2017 related to a machine guarding bracket failure. That loss was modest in size ($32,750 incurred) and appears to have been handled proactively. The insured implemented meaningful corrective actions (engineering review, standardized design guidelines, improved documentation) that directly address the root cause. No repeat or pattern losses are evident, and the more recent years are clean or limited to minor, fully resolved incidents.

Products/completed operations exposure is primarily B2B industrial components and subassemblies, which historically tend to be more controllable than consumer-facing products. There is no exposure to aircraft, automotive safety systems, medical implants, or other high-hazard/long-tail sectors. Limited foreign sales (Canada only, ~5%) and no known exposure to EU/UK product liability regimes or sanctioned territories. No residential work or public-assembly construction.

Premises exposure is moderate for a fabrication shop: welding, CNC machining, forklifts, and loading docks. However, operations are contained within a modern industrial facility with segregated welding areas, ventilation, and standard housekeeping controls. Visitor traffic is low and controlled through a front-office check-in process. No significant on-site chemical storage; no plating or large-scale painting operations in-house.

Auto exposure is limited to two company-owned box trucks used for local deliveries within roughly a 150-mile radius. No radius or long-haul trucking exposure, and drivers are company employees rather than contractors. Recommend verifying MVR standards and confirming any telematics or driver safety programs in place at the full submission stage.

Given the favorable loss experience, strong management involvement, and clear post-loss corrective measures, this account appears to be an acceptable risk for primary GL with a reasonable products/completed operations exposure. Recommend quoting on an occurrence GL form with $1M / $2M limits, including products/completed operations, and considering a $10,000 per-occurrence deductible or SIR, consistent with the insured’s current program and stated tolerance. Umbrella of $5M appears appropriate given customer contract requirements, although we should confirm any individual customer contracts requiring higher limits.

Underwriting items to confirm at full submission:
- Obtain 5-year currently valued loss runs directly from incumbent carrier to validate self-reported history.
- Confirm exact legal entity structure and any DBA names used on contracts and invoices.
- Verify that no products are used in critical life-safety applications (e.g., fall protection, public-use structures, or medical support devices).
- Review copy of the current safety manual and lockout/tagout procedures, including documentation of safety training for shop employees.
- Confirm that any subcontracted services (e.g., plating, large-scale painting) are performed by insured vendors with adequate GL and products coverage and that written hold-harmless/indemnity agreements are in place.

Assuming satisfactory responses and clean loss runs, I would be comfortable supporting competitive primary GL and umbrella pricing, potentially maintaining or modestly improving upon the insured’s current 0.92 schedule credit, contingent on final review of operations and controls.
